front: What is the definition of an eigenspace for a linear transformation T: V -> V?
back: For a given eigenvalue λ of T, the eigenspace E_λ is the set of all vectors v in V (including the zero vector) such that T(v) = λv. It is the subspace consisting of all eigenvectors associated with λ and the zero vector.

front: How is the eigenspace E_λ for a matrix A formally written as the solution set of a homogeneous system?
back: E_λ = Null(A - λI), which is the null space of the matrix (A - λI). This means it is the set of all solutions x to the equation (A - λI)x = 0.

front: What important property defines an eigenspace as a subspace?
back: An eigenspace is a subspace of the vector space V. It is closed under vector addition and scalar multiplication because if v and w are eigenvectors for λ, then T(v + w) = T(v) + T(w) = λv + λw = λ(v + w), and T(cv) = cT(v) = c(λv) = λ(cv).

front: How is the geometric multiplicity of an eigenvalue defined?
back: The geometric multiplicity of an eigenvalue λ is the dimension of its corresponding eigenspace, E_λ. It is equal to the number of linearly independent eigenvectors associated with λ, which is given by nullity(A - λI).

front: For a linear transformation T, if λ₁ and λ₂ are distinct eigenvalues, what is the intersection of their eigenspaces, E_λ₁ ∩ E_λ₂?
back: The intersection of eigenspaces for distinct eigenvalues is the trivial subspace {0}. A nonzero vector cannot be an eigenvector for two different eigenvalues simultaneously.
